sentences.text: There was-- as I said before-- a friend of mine who went to a farmer was hacked to death, because he wanted-- he claimed some property that belonged to him.
I didn't want to have to take any chances.
I mean, therefore, I didn't go.
Yes.
There were many stories.
Many.
Of course, after the war, after we came back from the camp.
And, you now, people were not really demanding.
They wanted to find out whether they would get everything back, or if they want something back, and there was-- in many cases, they were killed.
Not-- not exactly.
Him I knew directly.
I didn't know the other people.
I just heard about it.
There was always antisemitism in Poland.
It was just the degree that we have to concern about.
But there's always antisemitism
Well, now or then?
Then was probably greater than now, because then was a part of it-- now are the children of those.
The question was [INAUDIBLE] posed, oh, you came back?
In other words, I didn't expect you to come back.
But you came back.
It means you are alive, gee.
How come you're alive?
That's more or less was the question.
Happy?
Never happy.
[LAUGHS] It was never happy.
Actually, when I came back to my hometown, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] from Krakow, I went back-- I went to my hometown for the first time.
And I went-- we lived on the fourth floor.
And I went upstairs, and I ring the bell.
And a person answered the phone-- a woman answered the phone.
And not answer the phone, pardon me.
Answered the-- the bell, the doorbell.
And she asked me a questions, what's it all about?
And I-- I heard from the tone of her voice that she was German, by the accent, by the Polish accent, she was German.
So I [INAUDIBLE] make believe that I made a mistake.
I says, pardon me.
I says, I made a mistake.
It's the wrong-- wrong door.
And I went-- I went to the city hall of my hometown.
And I produced my papers that I was just released from concentration, from Schindler.
And also, and I told them, I says, look, I lived here before and I was forcibly removed, and I would like to have my apartment back.
The person in charge says, look, Mr.
Wiener, the story is this-- if there's a Pole lives there, we can do nothing about it.
But if a German lives there, you will have your apartment back.
Therefore, he says, come back tomorrow morning.
If the apartment is empty, you'll get the key.
He says, what would you like to have in case if there is a non-Polish family live there?
I says, look, I'd like to-- the furniture's mine, what I saw there.
I said, I'd like to have some bedding, some kitchen utensils.
That's all.
I don't want none of their property.
Well, I came next morning and I had the key.
And I-- the apartment was mine, back.
And many people, Jewish people who came back from camps, all from Russia, lived in my apartment.
It was like a sort of a hotel throughout my-- my living there.
In the meantime, my uncle, my mother's youngest brother, came from Russia and he lived there-- there.
And Sally, in the meantime, lived with her sister and my brother-in-law, and they moved to Lodz.
It's probably the second-largest city in Poland at that time.
So I went there and I said, Sally, let's come here, I says.
You know, and we'll see what's happening.
And she came there, and we were there together.
And when we want to get married, they told us, you have to wait a month, because there are many instances where people want to get married, and one of the spouses came back, is alive.
[INAUDIBLE] you get married, and they're alive, he says.
You have to put a note in papers, sort of advertisement, this-and-this party wants to get married, with no objections on anybody's part.
In the meantime, there was killing in Poland of Jews-- mass killings.
I says, Sally, I don't care what's happening.
I'm leaving Poland.
It's enough.
It's enough of Jewish blood spilled here, out.
We decided to leave.
So we got a pass from some Russians who were in charge.
And they gave us a pass.
He says, how far you want to go?
I says, what's the furthest you can give me?
He said, Trest.
I says, Trest, I will take.
So we crossed the German-- the border to Czechoslovakia.
The guy couldn't read Russian anyway, so he just saw Trest.
He says, all right.
So we passed by.
And we went to Czechoslovakia.
We went to Prague.
And we stayed there for only-- nobody could-- you couldn't get permanent residence in Prague, because everybody-- this was-- too many people who are there.
So you just got one week permanent residence, then you had to leave.
In Chorzow, we have been-- '45-- maybe two, three months.
Two, three months, no more.
[SIGHS] We were-- I think were buying certain things in Chorzow.
And we went up north and selling over there.
You know, not-- not too much.
Not too much business, really.
It wasn't-- wasn't a normal business anyway.
So--
My uncle remained there.
Yes.
With everything in it.
He stayed-- he stayed in Chorzow for a while.
And a lot of-- a lot of my people knew him.
They lived in my apartment.
I didn't know that they were there, you know.
And he eventually sold the apartment, and then we found out that he moved someplace else.
Because we-- when we moved to Czechoslovakia, the Czech-- because I had the pass, I was rehabilitated in-- in Brunnlitz, told us that if we move someplace else, they will give me an apartment.
So we moved.
We were four couples together, at that time.
So we moved to Usti nad Labem.
It's a-- a company that produces [?
Shift ?] soap.
It's a very well-known soap in-- in-- in Europe at that time.
So we went there, and we were-- we were introduced to a Russian captain, who happened to be Jewish.
So when he found out that we were Jews, they gave us a beautiful apartment with crystals, everything.
Must have been German apartment.
And we-- we stood there.
And in the meantime, I remembered I had an uncle in America, my father's oldest brother.
And the different between both of them was at least 18 to 20 years spread between ages.
And I wrote to him a letter.
I wrote to him in Polish, one letter, separately in German, and another separate, I know a couple words in-- in English.
Because in my high school, I took up English, But I didn't remember much when this.
I said, Sally, whatever it is, we have to have a permanent residence.
So we stay here until we get an answer.
After four weeks, we got an answer.
And my uncle wrote, whatever I do, I can't do nothing where you are now.
You have to move to US zone in Germany.
There we have US representative.
We could do something.
We bring you over here.
That day we got the letter, that's the day we left Czechoslovakia.
In the meantime, while we in Czechoslovakia, I worked in the [?
Shift ?] factory.
I didn't know work really, that [INAUDIBLE] because I played on a-- on a soccer team, of the factory soccer team.
So I just-- I was just-- token work.
But mostly with this, and that's how we make a living there.
Then we move to US zone in Germany.
We--
Usti nad Labem.
That's what's the name of this.
We were-- and then from-- from Czechoslovakia, we went to US zone in Germany.
We passed by the town of Hof, H-O-F.
Actually speaking, we were in about a group of about maybe 100 people when we crossed the border.
When we crossed the border, must've been some chalutzim that-- that helped us to cross the border.
It was not legal.
And the-- the border got both of them.
The German and the-- well, Germans was like Americans.
And the Czech looked the other way because we just crossed the border.
Went to US zone in Germany.
And this was actually the worst time in the postwar times, because there was no place, what to live.
We went to the DP camp, who was so overcrowded that it was deplorable.
It was in Vernwald was the name.
And we stood there only for a while.
This was '45.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Fall, '45.
Fall, '45.
And somebody told us that they're opening a new camp in Furth, by Nuremberg.
I said, we go there.
I said, whatever it is, it's better than here.
It was too overcrowded.
We went there.
And as soon as we came there, we said-- we applied for a marriage license.
And we could've got-- we could've got married, actually, in December, '45.
But I remember that my parents married in January 1, 1920.
I just happened to remember.
In the meantime, I got the message that my uncle, my mother's next younger brother, came from Czechoslovakia.
In other words, one-- one uncle lived in Poland.
His brother came also from-- [INAUDIBLE] from Russia.
So I said to Sally, I said, Sally, I'll write for his blessings.
I said, I have no parents, nobody.
So I left Sally in the DP camp in [?
Furth ?] and I went to Czechoslovakia.
And I met him, and I got his blessing.
So then I came back, and we got married January 1-- January 20, 1946.
Was it hard to travel back and forth?
Yes.
It was hard.
But the pass helped, to Czechoslovakia.
I was liberated there.
They gave me pass over there that I could travel freely, don't arrest me.
So I produced the pass so I was all right.
So we were  married there.
It was the first marriage in the DP camp, in this DP.
Our-- our wedding was the first wedding.
And then we had to go in February, to a civilian marriage also.
So in the-- in the city of Furth.
We got married over there.
We just pulled two people as witnesses, [LAUGHS] off-- off the street.
We got married there.
And then we waited for affidavit.
And we lived on [INAUDIBLE] cards, and I played ball over there.
So I got some extra cigarettes, what-- for playing or for winning.
And some table tennis tournaments also.
And we got a message towards the end of '46, that the papers are ready.
So we-- it was after Sukkos.
I remember it was after Sukkos.
So we went there to-- the-- the point was in the Funk Kaserne in Munich, that everybody congregated over there.
And from there, we shipped to-- to Hamburg, Germany.
And from there, we had to take a ship.
But in the meantime, we were stuck in Bremerhaven because there was a strike here [LAUGHS] in the United States, something new.
And we couldn't leave for about oh, two months, maybe, or more.
Finally, on January 3rd, we sailed from Bremerhaven.
We came on the 16th.
Yes.
Yeah.
Yeah, sure.
Of course.
Well, it wasn't like now, you couldn't get everything.
But [INAUDIBLE], whatever they provided, that's what we had.
Yeah.
So you know, as much as possible.
It wasn't all the [INAUDIBLE] and everything.
But it was-- it was the big one Sukkah in the middle.
And everybody attended the Sukkah, you know, as much as possible.
And we had the rabbi over there who conducted all the ceremonies.
And this was the first encounter, really, after the war, with the religious aspect of it.
Because during the war, there was not really-- I don't care what anybody says.
There was no-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [SIGHS] how do you say it?
No miracles.
I wish-- I wish there were.
But there was no miracles during the war.
Everybody was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] but everybody lived with the hope.
With all due respect to everything, everybody lived with the hope that [INAUDIBLE] persevere, not him.
[INAUDIBLE] persevere.
And then which we did.
Those who are alive persevered.
Unfortunately, those who didn't, didn't.
This was the-- the tragedy of the whole thing, that-- let's say, like, I had from my family, I persevered, my cousin, my uncle that lived in Vizhnitz, one also persevered.
Then there was a Monderer-- my family Monderer-- also one persevered, from the whole family.
We were the smallest, with three children.
Everybody has five or six or four.
And usually one only persevered.
And one from my aunt, what we loved her so much, none of them persevered.
And she, I would give 100% that she was-- you know, that she would see to it that all of them would be alive.
And none of them persevered.
You know?
It's-- but Polish Jews, who suffered the longest, because we suffered right away, 1939.
Whereby Hungarian Jews, 1944.
And they should have known what we went through.
But they didn't want to believe.
I speak to them because I worked in a factory with them.
And they didn't want to believe it.
You know, that's the tragedy of it.
They wouldn't believe it.
Not they-- not everybody would.
But more of them would-- would-- would be alive, really.
And by the Polish Jews, there was no way for us to escape.
I mean, there was some individual cases.
But as a-- as a total group, where would we go?
Well, you know the map of Poland.
Where could you go?
To Germany?
No.
To Austria?
No.
To Hungary?
Hungary's the only place at that time, in the beginning.
To Romania?
To Russia?
Well, Russia you couldn't go because Russia was a state of war.
So was Romania.
Hungary, you could have, if you have proper papers.
But it's not like this-- you buy papers and you go, you travel.
Every-- whatever-- every point was a danger of death.
Especially, you know, especially males.
You know?
And if you had a Semitic look, you had no chance.
Not that you were afraid of the Germans.
No, you were afraid of the Poles more than the Germans.
They would point out to you.
That's the tragedy of it.
Yeah, we came to the United States on 16th of January, 1947.
And my uncle and his daughter, my cousin, waited for us, who I've never met before.
And we waited almost all day.
Everybody was [INAUDIBLE] [LAUGHS] because everybody was-- came on the-- on the auspices of either joining the [INAUDIBLE] committee, or HIAS, or some other organizations.
[INAUDIBLE] we came private, so to speak.
We were the [YIDDISH].
[LAUGHS] We were private, but eventually we made it, you know?
And [INAUDIBLE] we took the cab to Penn Station.
As a matter of fact, I was passing by.
So I says, uncle-- because Jack Dempsey Russell.
And I said, uncle, this is the Jack Dempsey?
He says, you know about him?
I says [LAUGHS] yeah, I know about him.
So we went to Penn Station and we want to-- to an office by the Long Island Railroad.
And we lived there for about a month.
Yes, my cousin that-- they wanted us live longer there.
But I said-- I said, look, I have enough.
I says, I'm married now.
My wife is pregnant-- Sally was pregnant.
And I said, we lived for ourselves.
So my cousin, he didn't speak Yiddish.
She spoke Yiddish.
So she-- he tells her this-- tell him, they want-- he shouldn't rush so much to work.
Once he starts working, he never going to stop until he retire.
So what is his rush?
He says, he likes it.
He doesn't like it here?
So I told her, I says, it's OK, but it's a village.
I would like to live in town, city, New York.
I mean, skyscrapers, you know?
But you read in the papers, [LAUGHS] [? everything. ?] So he says, tell him again, if 15 years from now, he'll be able to live in a village like this, he'll do very well.
[LAUGHS] I just reminded, now, he was at our 50th anniversary.
And I remind him.
He says, well?
I said, you were right.
[LAUGHS] But they were very close together.
Yes.
He's American and she was born in Poland, but she came here as a child, my cousin.
My uncle passed away since-- in the '90s.
He helped us a lot, you know?
He helped us a lot to be sort of Americanized more than anything.
[LAUGHS] Where did you go after you left your uncle?
We went to live in the Bronx for about one month, in a furnished room.
And then we went to live in Brownsville, New York, you know.
My uncle paid about $250 to get an apartment.
At that time, you paid under the table to get an apartment.
And we got an apartment, and I came home one day and Sally was crying.
What's the matter?
She says, I saw rats over here.
I said, rats?
I says, yes.
I said, Sally, it's our apartment.
And we fixed it.
You know, everything is life.
You know, this is life.
You know, not everything is hunky dory, and all this.
Thank God, she gave birth to a healthy child.
Although Sally was not feeling well at all in the beginning, not at all.
She had to have, in the eighth month, a kidney operation, and she couldn't get out [? of the city ?] when she gave birth.
It was too dangerous for her.
And-- but everything came out all right.
Everything was fine.
The child was born.
And later on, they was telling me that an uncle was-- was here, and my cousins were here.
But they were not too many, because we came early.
There was no other-- Sally's sister was still in Germany at that time.
While we were living in Furth, we were visiting each other.
She lived in [INAUDIBLE], by-- by-- by Munich.
And every Jewish holiday, they-- we went there or they came to us.
You know, one of those things.
And we came here and thank God, her health got better, little by little.
And we made a life for ourselves and for our children.
I was always, for some reason, in the garment industry here.
I got-- what happened was this-- my cousin gave me a job through-- he had a hardware factory.
There was his father and his four-- three brothers.
There was four brothers.
And they had a factory, [INAUDIBLE].
So I didn't really want to work for him, for some reason or another.
So he gave me a job by one of his accounts, hardware industry.
And they were buying war surplus at that time.
And I was helping to unload the war surplus with somebody who knew it.
So after a month, I could already speak English.
English came to me really easy-- to me, easy.
Because I took English in school.
And I do-- actually, I wrote better than I spoke, at the beginning.
But-- so after a month, he gave me the job solely.
He says, you speak enough to check the papers and count it.
I'll need somebody else.
So means more work.
Anyway, so I worked there for a couple months.
And one day-- one Sunday, we were walking with the baby, you know, in the street, take a walk on Sunday.
And I see somebody was playing ball over there.
So I ask, who is playing?
So they says, Team Hatikvah.
It's a Jewish team.
So I said, where is the manager?
To the manager, I says, look, you need any players?
He says, you're a star also?
Everybody who comes here is a star.
I said, I'm not a star.
I says, you want me to play?
I said, I'll play.
Give me a pair of shoes, I'll play.
So he gave me a pair of shoes, and I played half a game.
And I scored two goals, and we were leading two-nothing.
And I get dressed, and I was going home.
I says, where you going?
I says, look, I just want to show that I know how to play.
And then we had a talk.
So I told him, I says, look-- he was a very nice guy, Mr.
Bernstein.
Very nice man, the manager.
So I said, look, I work in a place where I'm killing myself over there.
I says-- I says, I don't mind, but I don't make so much.
I  says, I'm looking for a job.
He says, listen to me.
The president of our club is in the garment industry, underwear.
He says, I'll get you a job by him.
I said, look, I would like to learn a trade while I'm there.
He said, OK.
So a day later, he introduced me.
We went there.
He introduced me to him.
He says-- he took me around.
He says, here, I make $35 a week.
He looked me around.
He says, OK, I'll start you at $40 a week.
He says, OK.
I says, oh, my wife and my child will be very happy.
He said, oh, you have a child too?
I says, yeah.
Well, no, then you have to have at least $45.
Make $10 more here.
And [INAUDIBLE] only 35 hour a week.
Over there, it was 40.
At that time, 1947, we had a 35-hour week.
So I went to him.
I says, look, the story is this and this.
I'll give you two weeks' notice, proper.
I spoke to my cousins.
I says, look.
She says, Harry, this is America.
You're not married to a job.
You could do better.
Give them a proper notice, and that's it, and leave.
[INAUDIBLE].
So I gave them a notice.
So I did, and then he wants to give me a raise.
I says, I gave work to the other guy already.
I can't do it anymore.
And that's how I wind up in the garment industry over there.
Did you find that people were aware that you were a survivor?
And how did you relate to the--
No, we never spoke about it.
I mean, they knew.
They knew I just came from Europe.
But I never, never spoke about it.
Never, to nobody.
We just, you know-- on our own group.
Yes, our own group, you relate.
But outsiders, no.
I never spoke to my cousins even.
Only if they asked me a question.
But I never so, quote-unquote, 'opened up.' It's-- it's-- I didn't feel like it was my own personal thing.
There's nothing-- even to my uncle, I didn't.
Just if he asked me questions, I answered.
But otherwise-- otherwise I wouldn't.
Even with my own children, it's very hard.
I know how somebody else.
And I mean, but I have a good relationship with them, thank God.
Knock wood.
You know?
But this is something else.
This is your [INAUDIBLE] inner thought.
That's the reason I took when somebody asked me.
I talked about Rabbi Mehlman.
And remember I was one in the shul-- one Saturday in the shul.
And the rabbi of-- leader of the synagogue had the sermon.
And one of his sermon, he relates that he was in the movie-- he saw a movie, And he says, a very hard movie to see.
But nevertheless, it's sort of duty for every Jew to see it, even if you don't like it.
But don't take children with you unless they are at least over 10 years of age or more.
OK, so I [INAUDIBLE].
He had the sermon.
And then he, after-- after the service was over, he had a Kiddush.
And so I offhandedly tell him.
I says, Rabbi Mehlman, I am one of the Schindler's List.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So he looks at me.
Doesn't say nothing, so I just walk away.
I mingle with other group.
Then he taps me on the shoulder.
He says, did I hear right, what you said?
That you were on the Schindler's List?
I says, yes.
He says, how come you didn't tell me?
I says, Rabbi, how is it proper?
You [INAUDIBLE].
I says, hold it a second!
I'm on Schindler's List.
So I wait, you finish talking.
I [INAUDIBLE].
So about 10 minutes later, he comes to me.
While he was-- I'm still there, he says, you know what I just spoke to?
One of our members.
Could you please address us, and the congregation?
On a breakfast, some Sunday, when we pick.
So I says, no, no.
It's my personal story.
I just wanted to tell you.
So he walks up to my wife.
She should persevere.
So you know, the woman [? does ?], usually.
So Sally just convinced me I should talk.
And that's how I thought it over, that it would be good if my children would be there.
So my children will be there, so this will be proper, for-- for me to talk.
And that's the reason I accept it at that time.
OK.
We're going to break here for the next tape.
